fuzzy_prd: Hey team, we need to wire up the OGNL expression evaluation runner for the upcoming demo. Basically we want a single entry point that reads a JSON blob from stdin, figures out what fixture to load (we have a handful of pre-baked ones — simple, sample, the array ones, list, etc.), evaluates the expression against it, and prints the result in that normalized two-line format we settled on. If there's a set_to field we should do the before/after thing like we discussed in the retro. Also make sure variables get seeded into the eval context before we run. For error cases just print the category label, no stack traces or anything noisy. One thing I keep forgetting to mention: the output format for numbers needs to match whatever we agreed on for the int-vs-float distinction — I think it's the same rule we used in the last formatter we shipped, so just check how that handles trailing zeros and the dot. Also the map result ordering was a pain point in QA last sprint so double-check that. The fixtures themselves need to match the sample graph from the existing test expectations, specifically the nested map structure, the disabled flag, the array contents, etc. We want the whole thing runnable as a standalone Java program fed JSON on stdin.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception

trigger_keywords: when does a number show a decimal point
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Integers (whole numbers with no fractional part) must be printed without a decimal point (e.g., result=11, result=256). Floating-point values must always include a decimal point and at least one fractional digit (e.g., result=2.5, result=1.0). The literal '1.' in source must render as 1.0 in output. Never print trailing zeros beyond one fractional digit unless they are significant.

Map results must be serialized with keys in ascending lexicographic (alphabetical) order. For example, #{"foo", "bar" : "baz"} renders as {"bar":"baz","foo":null} — 'bar' before 'foo'. This applies to all map construction results.

trigger_keywords: what does the sample fixture look like
what is in the sample object graph
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The supported fixture names are: none (null root), simple (a SimpleObject with isDisabled()=true, isThisVarArgsWorking()=true, getValueIsTrue(boolean)=passthrough), sample (a root object with a 'map' property containing: key 'size'=5000, key 'array'=int[]{1,2,3,4}, key 'list'=List containing elements [someObject, anotherObject, int[]{1,2,3,4}, 99, null] where [^] gives first=99... wait — map[^] gives 99 meaning the first key resolves to 99, map[$] gives null as the last entry; root also has 'disabled'=true), string_array (String[]{"hello","world"}), int_array (int[]{10,20}), list (a java.util.List with two elements, iterable).

When a set_to field is present in the input, the adapter must: (1) evaluate the expression to get the current value and print 'before_kind=<kind>' and 'before=<value>', (2) parse the set_to string as an OGNL expression to get the new value, assign it to the target, (3) re-evaluate the expression to get the updated value and print 'after_kind=<kind>' and 'after=<value>'. No 'kind=' or 'result=' prefix is used in this mode.

trigger_keywords: what to print on parse failure
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: On any error (parse/syntax failure or evaluation exception that indicates a malformed expression), print exactly: 'error=syntax\n'. Do not print stack traces, exception messages, or any other lines. The only supported error category label in output is 'syntax'.

trigger_keywords: what values are valid for kind
how to determine the kind field
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The 'kind' field must be one of: 'number' (any numeric value — Integer, Long, Double, Float, etc.), 'string' (java.lang.String), 'boolean' (Boolean), 'null' (null value), 'list' (java.util.List or OGNL list), 'array' (any Java array type), 'map' (java.util.Map). String values in results must be wrapped in double-quotes (e.g., result="hello world"). Boolean values are lowercase (true/false). Null prints as null.

trigger_keywords: how are variables named in the context
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The JSON input may contain a 'variables' object. Each key-value pair in that object must be placed into the OGNL evaluation context using the key name as-is (without a # prefix) — OGNL accesses them in expressions with the # prefix (e.g., variable key 'name' in JSON becomes accessible as #name in the expression).

The @@ prefix syntax (e.g., @@max(3,4)) invokes static methods on java.lang.Math via OGNL's default class resolution. Ensure the OGNL context is configured to allow access to java.lang.Math or that the default class for @@ expressions resolves to Math. The result of @@max(3,4) must be kind=number, result=4.

trigger_keywords: difference between list and map construction
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Curly-brace expressions without a leading # are list literals: {1,2,3} or {'a','b'}. Expressions with a leading # are map constructors: #{"key":"value"}. In a map constructor, a key listed without a colon (e.g., #{"foo", "bar":"baz"}) creates an entry for that key with a null value. The kind for curly-brace lists is 'list'; for #{} expressions it is 'map'.

trigger_keywords: caret and dollar in map index
first and last element of map
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For OGNL map/list access: [^] selects the first element and [$] selects the last element. In the sample fixture's 'map', map[^] returns 99 (first value) and map[$] returns null (last value). These are OGNL's first/last index operators on ordered collections.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the same rule we used in the last formatter we shipped, so just check how that handles trailing zeros and the dot
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The number rendering rule in the output formatter: integers print without decimal point, floats always include decimal point with at least one digit (e.g., 1.0 not 1.), matching the expected outputs in feature1_literals.json and feature2_arithmetic_logic.json

context_pointers.vague_reference: the map result ordering was a pain point in QA last sprint so double-check that
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Map keys in serialized output must be sorted in ascending lexicographic order, as demonstrated in feature6_composite_construction.json: #{"foo", "bar" : "baz"} => {"bar":"baz","foo":null}

context_pointers.vague_reference: the nested map structure, the disabled flag, the array contents
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The 'sample' fixture object graph as implied by feature4_1_property_navigation.json: root has 'disabled'=true, 'map' property is a Map with key 'size'=5000, 'array'=int[]{1,2,3,4}, 'list'=List with 5 elements where index 2 is int[]{1,2,3,4}, first key value is 99, last key value is null

context_pointers.vague_reference: the before/after thing like we discussed in the retro
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The set_to assignment output format in feature4_2_collection_array_access.json: four lines printed as before_kind=, before=, after_kind=, after= with no 'result=' prefix

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect documented right now — could you check the existing test case JSON files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the expected input/output contract, and look at the PRD in start.md for the behavioral description? If it's still unclear after that, flag it and we can sync.

feature1_literals.json.description: Literal parsing returns typed scalar and list values.

feature2_arithmetic_logic.json.description: Arithmetic, comparison, membership, and conditional expressions produce evaluated values.

feature3_context_variables.json.description: Context variables can be read, assigned, and reused inside one expression.

feature4_1_property_navigation.json.description: Nested properties, map entries, index aliases, and conditional property expressions resolve against a sample object graph.

feature4_2_collection_array_access.json.description: Array and collection roots expose length, iterator, indexed access, and assignment with conversion.

feature5_projection_selection.json.description: Projection and selection operators transform arrays and lists into selected result lists.

feature6_composite_construction.json.description: List, map, and array construction expressions create structured values with converted element values.

feature7_methods_static_constructors.jso.description: Expressions may invoke accessible instance behavior, static behavior, and constructors as part of evaluation.

feature8_errors.json.description: Malformed or invalid expressions are reported with normalized language-neutral error categories.
